title: Content Marketing Agent
description: Monitors trending topics across Hacker News, Dev.to, and Reddit, then drafts and schedules social media threads via Typefully. Tracks engagement and optimizes posting times.

conversation.content:
📊 Weekly Content Report — Apr 28

**Top Performing Thread:**
"Why every AI agent framework is rebuilding the same 5 things" — 847 impressions, 42 engagements, 12 link clicks

**This Week:**
• 3 threads published (Mon, Wed, Fri)
• 2,340 total impressions (+18% WoW)
• 89 total engagements
• Best time slot: Tuesday 9am EST

**Trending Topics Found:**
• "MCP servers are the new APIs" — HN front page, 234 points
• "Why RAG is dead" — Dev.to, 1.2k reactions
• Claude Code launch — Reddit r/programming, 890 upvotes

⚠ Instagram engagement down 12% — consider visual-first content this week.
Draft a thread about the MCP topic

🧵 Here's a draft thread on MCP servers:

**Tweet 1 (hook):** MCP servers are replacing REST APIs for AI agents. Not because REST is bad — because agents need tools, not endpoints. Here's the difference 🧵

**Tweet 2:** A REST API says: "here are my endpoints, figure out what to call." An MCP server says: "here are my capabilities, tell me what you need." Same data, completely different interface.

**Tweet 3:** The shift: instead of your agent parsing OpenAPI specs and guessing which endpoint to hit, MCP gives it typed tools with descriptions. The agent just calls `search_issues` instead of figuring out `GET /repos/:owner/:repo/issues?state=open&labels=bug`.

**Tweet 4:** This matters because error rates drop. Agents don't hallucinate endpoint paths. And when a tool changes, the server updates — the agent doesn't need new prompt engineering.

**Tweet 5 (CTA):** We built our entire agent runtime on this pattern. Every connection is an MCP server or a REST-to-MCP bridge. Agents get tools, not endpoints.

What do you think about this — want to hook or build the thread body differently?

📎 I'll generate a header image once you approve.
